## about_me

My name is Ashish Gaurav. I'm based in Augsburg and currently enrolled in the Master's International Business & Finance program at TH Augsburg, where I'm focused on M&A, valuations, and corporate finance.

Before Germany, I spent four years as a finance analyst — first inside the revenue accounting team at Tata Consultancy Services, supporting Lufthansa's DACH region, and then at American Express Global Business Travel. At Amex I worked closely with senior finance stakeholders on forecasting, variance analysis, and travel spend optimisation, building the reports and trackers they relied on cycle after cycle and managing the relationships that kept those workflows running. Both roles sat inside strict finance and control frameworks for large, regulated clients, and both taught me the same thing in different ways: finance, at its core, is about being able to trace every number back to a source and defend it.

That same instinct — building structured systems, maintaining clean records, and presenting the answer in a form someone else can act on — is what I've carried into everything since, including a side project I'm building called Let's Go.

Outside of finance, I travel whenever I can, and most of what I'm curious about right now lives at the intersection of markets, founders, and how good businesses actually get built.

---

## about_lets_go

**Coders have GitHub. Musicians have Reverb. Runners have Strava. Travellers — the ones whose entire identity is shaped by where they've been and where they're going next — have nothing.**

They're scattered across Instagram, Reddit threads, and group chats. Performing for the wrong audience, or whispering to the wrong friends. I've felt this gap for years without naming it. When I moved to Germany, I finally did.

That's what I'm building **Let's Go** to close. Three things sit at the centre of it:

- **A profile built on where you've been, not who's watching.** Horse riding across the Kazakh steppe. Climbing to Annapurna Base Camp. Tomorrowland last summer. *That* is a traveller's real story — not likes, not followers. Build a profile around it and the right people become findable: the woman planning her first solo trip finds someone who's done it, the climber in Munich finds a partner for the Annapurna attempt, the festival traveller finds her group for Tomorrowland next year instead of going alone or not going at all.

- **A home for the knowledge that usually dies in a Google Doc.** Travellers spend 100 to 200 hours planning serious trips — the route through the Pamir Highway, the visa workaround for Bhutan, the right month to surf Mentawai. They test the plan on the ground, learn what actually works, and then the entire thing disappears into a folder no one ever opens again. Let's Go turns that planning into something other travellers can find, trust, and build on — and turns the people who did the work into experts who can earn from what they know, without needing to become content creators.

- **Trust that comes from communities, not star ratings.** Reviews are gamed — every five-star liveaboard in Indonesia and every "best trek in Nepal" tour operator looks identical online. Communities aren't. The divers who've done a hundred dives know which Komodo operators skip safety briefings. The trekkers who've actually summited know which Kilimanjaro outfitters underpay porters and which ones don't. The riders who've crossed Mongolia know which guides will leave you stranded and which will get you home. That knowledge already exists — it just lives in private WhatsApp groups and forum threads no one can find. We're building the place where it surfaces, and once it does, a marketplace for guides, consultations, and local services can sit on top of it that no aggregator can match.

The bigger picture is a knowledge economy for travellers — where the person who rode across Kazakhstan earns from what they learned, where YouTube becomes the trailer and Let's Go becomes the movie, and where every cinematic travel video on the internet has a guide attached to it that turns inspiration into an actual plan.

It's early. Pre-MVP, scoping the first version around the identity and community pillars, and pursuing German non-dilutive grants (EXIST, BayTOU) before any company registration. Building it has been the most useful complement to my finance studies I could have asked for — it's where the spreadsheets meet reality.

External link: [onletsgo.com](https://onletsgo.com)

## work_experience

### role_1

title: Financial Analyst
company: American Express Global Business Travel
dates: Mar 2024 – Oct 2025
location: Gurugram, India

paragraph:
At Amex GBT I worked with senior finance teams at client companies, helping them run their monthly budgeting and forecasting cycles and find ways to bring travel spend down. Most of my time went into building the Excel models, Power BI dashboards, and PowerPoint decks that showed where forecasts were drifting, which costs were quietly creeping up, and which assumptions needed to change. The reports weren't one-offs — they were templates clients used month after month, so they had to be clean enough for someone else to refresh without me in the room. A lot of the job was also about the relationships: staying close to client controllers through Salesforce, pulling the right data out of SAP Concur, and presenting it in a form they could actually use.

bullets:
- Built recurring forecasting and variance analysis models designed to be refreshed cycle after cycle
- Owned client-facing reports and decks, turning raw expense data into recommendations senior stakeholders could act on
- Managed day-to-day relationships with client controlling teams on cost-centre mapping, reporting accuracy, and review cadence

### role_2

title: Revenue Analyst
company: Tata Consultancy Services (Lufthansa account)
dates: Oct 2021 – Feb 2024
location: Gurugram, India

paragraph:
My first finance job was inside the revenue accounting team for Lufthansa's DACH region — the team that makes sure every fare and tax flows correctly into the books. The work was unglamorous on paper: Excel reconciliations, chasing discrepancies through SAP ERP, supporting month-end close. But the environment taught me something I still rely on. Every number had to be traceable, every exception had to be explained in a clean PowerPoint summary, and every closing entry had to survive an audit trail. It's where I learned that finance isn't really about the model — it's about whether you can defend every cell in it, and explain it clearly to the person who has to sign it off.

bullets:
- Ran monthly reconciliations feeding into closing entries that touched both income statement and balance sheet positions
- Investigated fare and tax discrepancies, traced them to root cause across multiple ticketing and audit systems, and coordinated fixes with the right teams
- Worked directly with Lufthansa's finance controls and audit teams, holding to the turnaround and audit-trail standards a regulated client expects

### role_3

title: TOPSIM General Management Simulation
company: TH Augsburg
dates: Present-2027
location: Academic project

paragraph:
As part of the MSc program I owned the Financing and Planned Figures workstream for a multi-period business simulation, running a fictional B2B hardware company across a 10-year horizon. The model was a fully integrated three-statement build — P&L, balance sheet, and cash flow linked through assumption sheets that drove production volumes, pricing, financing mix, and ROE targets. After the first period the team came in at C+, which forced me to pull the plan apart, find the assumptions that were quietly wrong, and rebuild around them. The next period we landed at B+, which was less about the model getting better and more about learning to question the inputs harder.

bullets:
- Built linked P&L, balance sheet, and cash flow projections with assumption-driven scenario logic
- Diagnosed variances between actuals and plan each round, and revised assumptions accordingly
- Restructured the Period 1 financial plan after identifying weak underlying assumptions
